findings.evidence: `TorusFundamentalGroup.lean:41-45` uses `(0, 0)` and `fundamentalGroupMulEquiv_zero`; `CircleFundamentalGroup.lean:207-209` has `fundamentalGroupMulEquiv (hp : p ≠ 0) {x : AddCircle p} (e : ((↑) : ℝ → AddCircle p) ⁻¹' {x}) : FundamentalGroup (AddCircle p) x ≃* Multiplicative ℤ`.
findings.fix: Prove the general binary statement with `{x : AddCircle p} {y : AddCircle q}` and lifts `ex : ((↑) : ℝ → AddCircle p) ⁻¹' {x}`, `ey : ((↑) : ℝ → AddCircle q) ⁻¹' {y}`, using `fundamentalGroupMulEquiv p hp ex` and `fundamentalGroupMulEquiv q hq ey`; derive the zero-basepoint version as a specialization if desired.
findings.issue: `prodFundamentalGroupMulEquiv` only computes `FundamentalGroup (AddCircle p × AddCircle q) (0, 0)`, but the nearby circle API already supports any basepoint with a chosen lift.

findings.evidence: `TorusFundamentalGroup.lean:50-53` fixes `(fun _ => 0)` and calls `fundamentalGroupMulEquiv_zero`; `CircleFundamentalGroup.lean:207-209` supplies the more general per-coordinate equivalence.
findings.fix: State the indexed theorem for `{x : ∀ i, AddCircle (p i)}` with `e : ∀ i, ((↑) : ℝ → AddCircle (p i)) ⁻¹' {x i}`, then compose `FundamentalGroup.piMulEquiv x` with `MulEquiv.piCongrRight fun i => fundamentalGroupMulEquiv (p i) (hp i) (e i)`; make the current zero theorem a corollary.
findings.issue: `piFundamentalGroupMulEquiv` only computes the product of circles at the constant zero basepoint, although the component equivalence is already arbitrary-basepoint.

summary: The product formula declarations are at the natural indexed/binary product level, but the torus corollaries unnecessarily specialize to the zero basepoint despite the existing circle computation being available at arbitrary basepoints with chosen lifts.
